name: Lucky Strick and other mines in San Rafael district
short_description: Historic mining district on BLM land featuring copper and turquoise prospects. Public rockhounding allowed with 25 lb daily limit on this scenic high desert claim area.
description:
The Lucky Strick and associated mines in the San Rafael district represent some of Utah's most accessible historic mining terrain. Located in the heart of Emery County's mineral-rich zone, these claims sit on BLM public land where recreational collectors can legally prospect for copper minerals, turquoise, and trace gold without a permit. The San Rafael district was actively worked for copper and precious metals during the late 1800s and early 1900s, and abandoned mine shafts and tailings still contain colorful malachite, azurite, and chrysocolla specimens.

The geology here reflects the Laramide orogeny and Basin and Range extension, with copper mineralization occurring in altered granitic intrusions and sedimentary host rocks. Oxidized zones near the surface produce the bright blue and green copper carbonates that make this area visually distinctive and rewarding for collectors. The high desert elevation and sparse vegetation make the mineral exposures relatively easy to spot compared to more vegetated regions.

Rockhounds visiting the Lucky Strick district should expect rough terrain with scattered rock outcrops, collapsed mine structures, and weathered tailings piles. The combination of open vistas and accessible mineralized zones makes this a moderate-difficulty site suitable for experienced collectors and intermediate rockhounds willing to hike a few miles. Early morning visits are strongly recommended to avoid afternoon heat and to maximize visibility in the clear desert light.

While the site requires some effort to reach and navigate, the variety of copper minerals and the historical character of the mines make it a unique destination. Collectors should respect all posted claims, avoid disturbing active mining operations, and practice Leave No Trace principles. The remoteness and elevation offer solitude and genuine rockhounding adventure away from commercial fee dig sites.
history: The San Rafael district saw significant copper prospecting and mining from the 1890s through the 1950s, with the Lucky Strick and neighboring claims representing classic Basin and Range mineralization. Copper-rich fluids circulated through granitic batholiths and sedimentary rocks during the Laramide orogeny (80-55 million years ago), depositing primary sulfides that weathered into the vibrant secondary minerals visible in outcrops and mine tailings today.
tips: Search the weathered tailings piles and oxidized outcrop faces near old mine workings for bright green malachite and blue azurite specimens - these secondary copper minerals are most visible on freshly exposed surfaces. Use a 10x loupe to identify fine turquoise and chrysocolla deposits in fractures within the host rock, and bring a cold chisel and hammer to carefully extract small hand samples from loose talus rather than attempting to dig into solid outcrops. Early morning light makes the colored minerals easier to spot against the tan desert rock, and always stay alert for unstable mine entrances and loose rock on steep slopes.
terrain_notes: Elevation approximately 6,800 feet with high desert terrain featuring exposed bedrock, scattered sagebrush, and minimal shade. Walking distance from parking to prime collecting areas ranges from 0.5 to 2 miles depending on which claims you explore. Footing is uneven with loose talus, sharp angular rock fragments, and occasional steep slopes. The terrain is rocky but not heavily vegetated, making navigation relatively straightforward despite the lack of maintained trails.
best_season: April - May and September - October. Spring offers mild temperatures and spring rains that can expose fresh mineralized surfaces, while fall provides comfortable hiking conditions. Summer heat (over 90 degrees F) makes midday collecting difficult, and winter snow at this elevation can limit access some years.
written_directions: From Castle Valley, drive east on UT 128 toward the San Rafael Swell. Continue approximately 18 miles to the signed turnoff for San Rafael mining district roads. Take the rough dirt road northeast for approximately 8-10 miles, watching for historic mine markers and claim signs. Park at one of the wide pullouts near Lucky Strick or neighboring mines. A high-clearance or 4x4 vehicle is recommended, especially after rain when clay-based dirt roads become slippery. GPS coordinates 38.755292, -110.948502 mark the general district center. Be prepared for slow travel on unimproved roads and carry extra water and a tire repair kit.
road_conditions: Rough unimproved dirt roads with washboard texture and occasional deep ruts. High-clearance or 4x4 recommended. Roads may become impassable after heavy rain when clay-rich soils become slick. No regular maintenance.
parking_notes: Informal pullouts and small clearings near mine sites. No developed lot or facilities. Multiple parking areas available along the main access road within the district. Plan for 3-4 vehicles maximum at each location.
collection_rules: Personal use only. Maximum 25 lbs per day per FLPMA regulations. No commercial collection or motorized excavation. No explosives or heavy equipment use. Respect all posted mining claims - do not trespass on private claims. Avoid disturbing mine structures and do not enter unsafe shafts or adits. Pack out all trash and leave the area undisturbed for future collectors.
rules:
Personal use only
Maximum 25 lbs per day

No motorized excavation
Respect posted mining claims
No entry into unsafe mine structures
Pack out all trash
Avoid heavy equipment use
nearest_services: Castle Valley (approximately 20 miles west) has limited services. Green River, Utah (approximately 35 miles north) offers gas, food, water, and lodging. Emery County Sheriff dispatch is the nearest emergency contact.
accessibility_notes: Uneven rocky terrain with steep slopes near some mines. Footing is loose and unpredictable with sharp angular rock fragments throughout. No wheelchair access or maintained trails. Sturdy hiking boots and careful footing essential. Not suitable for limited mobility. Remote location requires self-sufficiency and advance planning.

faq.question: What copper minerals can I find at Lucky Strick and other San Rafael district mines?
faq.answer: The primary secondary copper minerals found here include bright green malachite, deep blue azurite, and blue-green chrysocolla. These oxidized minerals form near the surface where primary copper sulfides have weathered over millions of years. Specimen quality ranges from small crystals to larger polishable pieces, depending on the specific claim and exposure you explore.

faq.question: Do I need a permit to rockhound at the Lucky Strick mines in Utah?
faq.answer: No permit is required for recreational collecting on this BLM land. You can legally collect personal use specimens under FLPMA regulations without advance permission. However, you must respect all posted mining claims and stay within the 25 lb per day personal collection limit. Always verify current claim status before visiting specific mines.

faq.question: How far is the drive from Castle Valley to the San Rafael mining district?
faq.answer: It is approximately 20 miles from Castle Valley to the Lucky Strick mines, taking about 45 minutes to one hour via UT 128 and rough mining district roads. The final 8-10 miles are on unimproved dirt roads requiring high-clearance or 4x4 vehicles, especially after rain. Plan for slow travel and bring extra water and tire repair supplies.

faq.question: What is the best time of year to rockhound at San Rafael for copper specimens?
faq.answer: April through May and September through October are ideal. Spring offers mild temperatures and rains that expose fresh mineralized surfaces, while fall provides comfortable hiking in clear desert conditions. Summer heat exceeds 90 degrees F and makes midday collecting difficult. Winter snow at 6,800 feet elevation can limit access.

faq.question: Is the Lucky Strick mine area beginner-friendly for rockhounding in Utah?
faq.answer: This site is best suited for intermediate to experienced rockhounds. The terrain is rough and remote with steep slopes, loose talus, and abandoned mine hazards. You need high-clearance vehicle and sturdy hiking boots. Beginners should start at easier fee dig sites and gain experience before attempting this remote BLM location.

faq.question: What tools and safety gear do I need for rockhounding the San Rafael mining district?
faq.answer: Bring a cold chisel, hammer, 10x loupe, sturdy hiking boots, hat, and plenty of water (minimum 2 liters). A headlamp is useful for examining dark fractures. Essential safety items include a first aid kit, tire repair kit, extra fuel, and a GPS device or map. Because this is a remote area with no cell service, tell someone your planned route and expected return time.

meta_description: Find malachite, azurite, and copper specimens at Lucky Strick mines in the San Rafael district. BLM public land, no permit required, 25 lb daily limit.
